## 2026-01-11 Update

Resolution events:
- BRICS naval exclusion resolved to Iran. I wrongly treated "all members invited" and visible participation as exclusion-protective. Lesson: for narrowly worded "reportedly ordered excluded" markets, do not overrule the reported exclusion clue just because early drill coverage lists participation.
- Maskana/Deir Hafer armed-organization and eastern-Aleppo armed-group markets resolved to Kurdistan Workers' Party/PKK, not SDF. Lesson: when Syrian official/security reporting talks about Kurdish fighters, keep PKK/Kurdistan Workers' Party live even if international coverage uses SDF.
- Manchester United derby manager resolved to Michael Carrick, which our interim-manager prior caught.
- Somalia agreement/separatist-support country markets resolved to UAE, not Israel. Lesson: Somaliland recognition coverage can be noisy; older UAE/Somaliland patronage remains a strong country prior unless the question is explicitly about Israeli recognition.
- Senate funding department resolved to Department of Homeland Security, matching the DHS/ICE appropriations signal.

Updates made:
- Syria/Aleppo: Al Jazeera reported the last Kurdish-led SDF fighters left Aleppo, the governor said the city was empty of SDF fighters, and reporting said the Syrian army took over Sheikh Maqsoud. Raised Aleppo to near-certainty for both the Syrian-army-complete-control city and the SDF-assault target city.
- Gaza governance: Jan. 11 coverage said Trump is set to name the Gaza Board of Peace, with Nickolay Mladenov the only identified CEO/director figure, while Hamas/other reports described the body taking over Gaza as a Palestinian technocratic leadership committee. Raised Mladenov for the leadership/person market and raised Palestinian Technocratic Committee for the body-name market while keeping Board of Peace as the main alternative.
- DOJ/governor immigration market: the Dec. 23 DOJ lawsuit against Illinois Gov. J.B. Pritzker over courthouse-arrest protections is the cleanest "impede federal immigration operations" signal; Jan. 11 Minnesota coverage added Tim Walz risk because he is now central to the federal ICE/fraud confrontation. Raised Pritzker first and added Walz.
- Board of Peace South Asia: Bangladesh officially expressed interest in joining the Gaza international stabilization force. It is not formal Board membership, but it materially raises Bangladesh versus Pakistan for the only South Asian participant market.
- Iran/US bases: Jan. 11 coverage repeatedly said Iran threatened US bases and ships if attacked, and noted the prior Qatar base strike plus the Fifth Fleet in Bahrain. Raised Qatar and Bahrain for the future base-location market while keeping Iraq live.
- Pakistan/Bangladesh T20 dispute: Jan. 11 Hindustan Times said the Bangladesh T20 World Cup venue stalemate continues and the ICC verdict is expected Jan. 12 or 13. Raised Bangladesh as the country Pakistan would say it is supporting if it boycotts India.
- Venezuela oil/armada: CBS said US forces seized another Venezuela-linked tanker in the Caribbean. Raised Caribbean for the "big armada" region. Fox/other Venezuela oil coverage reinforced US accounts for oil revenue, but I added small Switzerland/UK tails because separate reporting highlighted frozen Venezuelan assets/gold there.

Watched but no update:
- Ukraine opposition bribery, pro-Israel New York settlement, Atletico Copa opponent, Southeast Asian visa pause, NE ICE crackdown state, food-aid halt, basketball-rigging league, migrant-worker custody death, Syria language/holiday, Marc Guehi, Ukraine combat planes, Indonesian plane operator, Karachi fire, Portugal runoff, and the Australian Open opponent markets had no direct Jan. 11 naming signal strong enough to move.
- Al-Shaddadi remains a Syrian army lean, but the Aleppo withdrawal does not directly identify who will control that base.

## 2026-01-12 Update

Resolution events:
- Ukrainian opposition bribery resolved to Yulia Tymoshenko. I overweighted Poroshenko and treated old opposition leaders as tails. Lesson: when the clue only says "opposition leader" in Ukraine, keep Tymoshenko materially live even if current reporting is thin.
- The pro-Israel New York settlement resolved to Betar US. I had Betar USA, which is likely an exact-string miss. Lesson: use the organization's exact published short name when known, even if a close variant is semantically obvious.
- Atletico Madrid Copa opponent resolved to Real Betis. I missed the draw by leaning Sevilla/Real Sociedad. Lesson: sports draw markets need the actual draw when available; otherwise include plausible domestic rivals like Betis, not only the biggest names.

Updates made:
- Marc Guehi: Sky said the race remains wide open and specifically added Bayern Munich as the viable foreign pre-contract route, while the Independent said Manchester City are actively pushing a January deal and Guehi is open to it. Kept City first but added Bayern as a serious hedge.
- Oscars nominations: Golden Globes coverage said One Battle After Another led the Globe nominations and won major film awards; Hamnet won drama and Sinners remains a high-nomination contender. Raised One Battle After Another and Paul Thomas Anderson in the linked Oscar markets while adding Sentimental Value/Joachim Trier as nonzero nomination-count hedges.
- Greenland framework: Jan. 12 coverage centered the upcoming Washington talks on Greenland FM Vivian Motzfeldt, Danish FM Lars Lokke Rasmussen, Mette Frederiksen's "decisive moment" warning, and Rubio. Kept Motzfeldt first, raised Mette and Lars slightly, and trimmed Jens-Frederik Nielsen.
- Gaza body name: AP-style Jan. 12 coverage used the phrase "Palestinian technocratic leadership committee" and said Hamas would dissolve its Gaza government once that body takes over. Added that exact wording above the shorter Palestinian Technocratic Committee to reduce exact-name risk, with Board of Peace still a main alternative because it oversees the body.

Watched but no update:
- Gaza committee leader: the same AP-style report said the technocrats' names have not been announced. Mladenov remains the only named director/CEO-type figure from Board of Peace reporting, but the article does not directly name the Palestinian committee leader.
- Iran escalation reporting was much hotter, with Trump saying the military is weighing strong options and Iran warning US bases/ships could be targets. It reinforces the existing Qatar/Bahrain/Iraq base-location priors but did not identify a specific base, talks venue, or strike site enough to change those detailed markets.
- NE ICE crackdown state, Southeast Asian visa pause, Massie/Venezuelan oil-proceeds country, Venus/Australian Open draw, UN food-aid halt, basketball-rigging league, migrant-worker custody death, Syria holiday/language, Ukraine combat planes, Indonesian plane operator, Karachi fire, Portugal runoff, Morocco missed penalty, Bangladesh election-party and T20-replacement markets had no stronger Jan. 12 naming signal.

## 2026-01-13 Update

Resolution events:
- NE ICE crackdown resolved to Maine. I had only New York/Massachusetts. Lesson: for northeastern enforcement-market tails, include Maine/New Hampshire/Vermont when the wording says "northeastern state" and not just the largest sanctuary-state fights.
- Gaza committee leader resolved to Ali Shaath. I missed by over-weighting Mladenov/Board of Peace and PA figures. Lesson: for "Palestinian technocratic committee" leadership, add less famous Gaza technocrats and local civil-administration names, not only national politicians or international overseers.
- Massie Venezuelan oil proceeds resolved to Qatar, not the United States. Lesson: offshore/escrow oil-money questions can resolve to mediator/gulf-finance states even when US control is the policy frame.
- Southeast Asian treaty-ally visa pause resolved to Thailand. Lesson: do not assume Philippines for every US treaty-ally immigration action; Thailand should be a core hedge.
- Gaza body name resolved to Palestinian national committee. I chased "technocratic leadership committee" and missed the simpler national-committee phrase. Lesson: for Gaza governance bodies, include "Palestinian national committee" alongside technocratic variants.

Updates made:
- Bangladesh cricket: Al Jazeera and Hindustan Times reported that the BCB reaffirmed it will not travel to India for T20 World Cup matches and asked the ICC to relocate Bangladesh's fixtures. Raised Bangladesh for the Pakistan-boycott-support market and raised UAE modestly for a possible Bangladesh replacement team.
- Gaza/Board of Peace participation: Hindustan Times reported Bangladesh raised joining the Gaza international force in State Department meetings and received a positive US response, with conditions attached; it also mentioned Pakistan participation. Raised Bangladesh for the only South Asian Board of Peace member and added Bangladesh to the later "up to 8,000 troops" Gaza-mission market.
- Minnesota/DOJ governor: Jan. 13 coverage had DHS directly demanding Gov. Tim Walz and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey honor ICE detainers, while Minnesota sued over the ICE surge and Trump attacked Walz. Moved Walz above Pritzker for the DOJ investigation/conspiracy-to-impede-enforcement market.
- Carrick/Manchester United: Carrick was confirmed as United interim head coach, with his first match the Manchester derby and Arsenal away soon after. Raised Manchester City and Arsenal for Carrick's first-defeat market.
- NFL playoffs: Eagles were out, and the NFC divisional field is Seahawks, Bears, 49ers and Rams. Removed Hurts/Eagles exposure, raised Sam Darnold, Caleb Williams, Brock Purdy and Matthew Stafford for the NFC Super Bowl quarterback market, and updated the Super Bowl winner set toward Seahawks/Broncos/Bears/Patriots/49ers.
- Greenland framework: Daily Beast and other coverage said JD Vance will host the White House meeting with the Danish and Greenland foreign ministers, while Greenland PM Jens-Frederik Nielsen emphasized NATO/Denmark. Raised Vivian Motzfeldt and Lars Lokke Rasmussen as direct-meeting counterparts while keeping Mette/Jens live.
- Syria/SDF assault: Jan. 13 Al Jazeera/SANA coverage said the Syrian army sent troops toward eastern Aleppo after observing SDF reinforcements near Maskana and Deir Hafer. Kept Aleppo first but added Maskana/Deir Hafer hedges for the assault-target city.
- Iran diplomacy/tariffs: Trump announced a 25% tariff on countries doing business with Iran, with reporting naming China first/top and also UAE/Turkey/India. Moved China above India for the tariff-country market. Qatari PM contact with an Iranian official and Doha de-escalation language raised Qatar/Doha modestly for Iran talks/intermediary markets, while Oman/Muscat remains first for formal indirect talks.
- Venus Williams: Sky reported Venus lost to Tatjana Maria at Hobart less than a week before the Australian Open. Added Tatjana Maria as a live hedge for the AO first-round defeater market, while recognizing the AO draw is not yet available.
- Iran World Cup participation: escalating protests, US citizen departure warnings, tariff threats and military-option coverage increase the chance Iran cites inability to participate or seeks venue changes. Raised Iran for the "cannot participate in World Cup" market, but kept the detailed venue/strike/base markets mostly unchanged without location-specific reporting.

Watched but no update:
- Jan. 14 basketball-rigging league, migrant-worker custody death, UN emergency food/nutrition aid halt, Ukraine combat-plane provider, Syria national language/holiday, UDP New Jersey primary target, Indonesian plane operator, Karachi fire and southern Spain train-collision markets still had no direct naming signal.
- Marc Guehi coverage repeated the Jan. 12 Sky state of play; no new club agreement signal beyond the existing City/Bayern/Liverpool/Arsenal distribution.
- Sudan RSF drone attack in Sinja killed 27 at an army base, but it did not match the later market wording about a crowded central-Sudan market killing at least 28.

## 2026-01-14 Update

Resolution events:
- "Big armada" resolved to Middle East, not Caribbean. I overweighted Venezuela/naval-seizure coverage and missed the Iran/US regional-buildup frame. Lesson: when wording is broad and US-Iran tension is hot, keep Middle East/Gulf as a core region even if Caribbean military coverage is vivid.
- Foreign basketball league targeted in the game-rigging scheme resolved to Chinese Basketball Association. I had it in the set but too low. Lesson: in US sports-corruption cases, Chinese Basketball Association is a strong foreign-league prior.
- Migrant-worker custody death resolved to El Hacen Diarra. I missed the exact name. Lesson: for custody-death/protest markets, include local/French-speaking West African names where available rather than only English-language protest names.
- UN emergency food/nutrition aid halt resolved to Somalia. I had Somalia but behind Sudan. Lesson: for WFP/UN emergency nutrition funding-shortage wording, Somalia should be near the top even when Sudan and Gaza dominate humanitarian headlines.
- Venus Williams AO first-round defeater resolved to Olga Danilovic. My Tatjana Maria warmup hedge did not transfer to the AO draw. Lesson: tennis draw markets need bracket names; do not overfit tune-up opponents before the draw.

Updates made:
- Syria/SDF: Jan. 14 reporting said the army's evacuation corridor appeared to signal an offensive in Deir Hafer and Maskana east of Aleppo. Moved the SDF assault target from Aleppo toward Deir Hafer/Maskana, and reduced but kept Aleppo for the complete-control follow-on market.
- Al-Shaddadi: the same pressure on SDF withdrawal/integration keeps Syrian army first for eventual base control, but SDF remains a meaningful holdout tail.
- Iran/Gulf bases: Al Udeid personnel in Qatar were advised to leave after Iran warned regional states that US bases could be attacked. Raised Qatar across the killed-troops, KC-135, launch-area, embassy-evacuation and missile-recipient markets, with Bahrain/UAE/Saudi Arabia as main alternatives.
- Strait/Hormuz conflict risk: the hotter Iran-US confrontation raises Strait of Hormuz/Gulf of Oman risk for projectile-hit cargo shipping.
- Greenland framework: White House meetings featured Greenland and Danish foreign ministers, so moved Lars Lokke Rasmussen up while keeping Vivian Motzfeldt first and Mette/Jens as hedges.
- League Cup: Arsenal beat Chelsea 3-2 and Manchester City beat Newcastle 2-0 in first legs. Raised Arsenal sharply as City's likely final opponent.
- Portugal: the Catolica poll showed Ventura 24, Antonio Jose Seguro 23, Joao Cotrim de Figueiredo 19, and Marques Mendes/Gouveia e Melo around 14. Moved Seguro first for the runoff opponent while keeping Cotrim and the two conservative/independent candidates live.
- DOJ/governor immigration market: Jan. 14 ICE/Renee Good coverage kept Minnesota and Tim Walz central to the federal confrontation, with DOJ still refusing/contesting investigations. Raised Walz over Pritzker.
- Marc Guehi: Jan. 14 transfer live coverage said he prefers Arsenal or Liverpool, City are desperate for January, and Bayern are interested. Kept City first but spread more to Liverpool/Arsenal/Bayern.
- Ukraine-Russia-US talks: reports and EU commentary kept US-led negotiations centered on Witkoff/Trump channels while Europe debates direct Russia engagement. Added Moscow as the leading city hedge for the Jan. 23-24 three-way-talks market, but kept US mediation first.

Watched but no update:
- Syria national language/holiday, Ukraine combat-plane provider, UDP New Jersey target, Indonesian plane operator, Karachi fire, Morocco missed-penalty final market, Australian Open draw markets, Gaza child evacuation, Board of Peace South Asian member, Gaza troop provider, tariff-country, ICE/Olympics, LA wildfire funds and Super Bowl markets had no Jan. 14 naming signal strong enough to move beyond existing distributions.

    print('\\nEV', ev.get('sim_date'), ev.get('qid'), ev.get('ground_truth'))

df=pd.read_csv('market.csv', dtype={'qid':str})

print('columns', list(df.columns))
print('\\nnewest resolved tail')
print(df[df.is_resolved].tail(12)[['qid','resolution_date','title','ground_truth','market_aggregate']].to_string(index=False, max_colwidth=140))
print('\\nnear active by resolution_date <= 2026-01-18')

PY"

Syrian army tells civilians to evacuate new front with SDF east of Aleppo

The Syrian army is telling civilians to evacuate parts of the rural Aleppo governorate after accusing the Syrian Democratic Forces (SDF) of building up troops in the area, following days of deadly fighting inside Aleppo city, as concerns rise over a new front in the simmering battle. A “humanitarian zone” was expected to remain open on Thursday from 9am local time (6:00 GMT) to 5pm (14:00 GMT), allowing residents to leave Deir Hafer and Maskana in the eastern countryside. 266:{"id": "fdfdf0132ca2cc5b3a0921a82e2ea33814b797a8033a58d7ab0760b50e071958", "title": "Australian Open draw: Emma Raducanu in line to face Aryna Sabalenka in third round again while Novak Djokovic in same half as Jannik Sinner", "source": "www.skysports.com", "date": "2026-01-15", "date_publish": "2026-01-15", "date_modify": null, "url": "https://www.skysports.com/tennis/news/12040/13494465/australian-open-draw-emma-raducanu-in-line-to-face-aryna-sabalenka-in-third-round-again-while-novak-djokovic-in-same-half-as-jannik-sinner", "content": "Emma Raducanu has been handed a tough path at the Australian Open despite being seeded, with world No 1 Aryna Sabalenka potentially awaiting in the third round.
Raducanu, who lost in the quarter-finals of the Hobart International on Thursday, is seeded 28th for the opening Grand Slam of the year and will face Thailand's Mananchaya Sawangkaew in the first round.
Austria's Anastasia Potapova or Netherlands' Suzan Lamens would be Raducanu's second-round opponent before a rematch of last year's Wimbledon clash against Sabalenka, which also came in the third round.
Latest scores, results and schedule from WTA and ATP Tours
Australian Open 2026: Dates, seeds, format and favourites
Not got Sky? Get Sky Sports or stream contract-free on NOW
Pre-tournament favourite Sabalenka will begin her campaign for a third Australian Open title against French wild card Tiantsoa Rakotomanga Rajaonah while six-time major champion Iga Swiatek will play a qualifier, with a potential fourth-round meeting against Naomi Osaka in the second week.
Britain's Katie Boulter, who was a last-minute entry into the main draw, has a tough opener against in-form 10th seed Belinda Bencic, while Sonay Kartal faces 31st seed Anna Kalinskaya and Francesca Jones will play a qualifier.
Venus Williams is set to become the oldest woman to compete in an Australian Open main draw, surpassing the record of 44 years old, previously held by Japan's Kimiko Date, and could face compatriot Coco Gauff if the American duo win their opening matches.
Djokovic in same half as Sinner as Britain's Fery qualifies for main draw
Novak Djokovic has been drawn in the same half as defending men's singles champion Jannik Sinner as the Serbian continues his quest for a 25th Grand Slam.
Djokovic will enter the Australian Open, which he has won a record 10 times, without playing competitive tennis since November after withdrawing from last week's Adelaide International.
The 38-year-old cut short a practice session in Melbourne due to a neck problem on Wednesday, raising concerns about his fitness.
As the fourth seed, he cannot face Sinner until the semi-finals - the stage he lost at each of last year's four majors in 2025 - and will take on Spain's Pedro Martinez in his first match.
Reigning champion Sinner goes up against France's Hugo Gaston while Carlos Alcaraz, who is looking to complete his career Grand Slam, plays Australia's Adam Walton.
Britain's Cameron Norrie is seeded 26th and takes on France's Benjamin Bonzi, before a possible third-round tie with third seed Alexander Zverev, while Jacob Fearnley will meet Poland's Kamil Majchrzak.
Norrie and Fernley will be join in the main draw by compatriot Arthur Fery, who will make his maiden Grand Slam appearance outside of Wimbledon.
Fery won all three of his qualifying matches with straight-set victories and comfortably saw off Croatia's Dino
Prizmic 6-4 6-1 on Thursday and will take on Italy's Flavio Cobolli in his opening match.
Big Australian home hope Alex de Minaur has a tough first-round draw against former Wimbledon finalist Matteo Berrettini.", "authors": "[]", "description": "Emma Raducanu could meet Aryna Sabalenka in the third round for the second time in a Grand Slam in the last seven months following the Australian Open draw; watch WTA and ATP action in Adelaide and Hobart International this week, live on Sky Sports Tennis"}
651:{"id": "e4b21c0d1161daa82f600676afd1247d9fe9e66b5d2e723e9f01b98fca861e61", "title": "Roger Federer hails Alcaraz and Sinner’s ‘great’ rivalry amid Australian Open return", "source": "www.irishtimes.com", "date": "2026-01-15", "date_publish": "2026-01-15", "date_modify": null, "url": "https://www.irishtimes.com/sport/tennis/2026/01/15/roger-federer-hails-alcaraz-and-sinners-great-rivalry-amid-australian-open-return/", "content": "Roger Federer hailed the dominance of Carlos Alcaraz and Jannik Sinner as the six-time champion returned to the Australian Open for the first time since his retirement in 2022.
Federer, who last travelled to Australia in 2020, will headline the inaugural opening ceremony with a doubles exhibition match alongside Andre Agassi, Patrick Rafter and Lleyton Hewitt.
“The rivalry with Alcaraz and Sinner is a great one,” said Federer. “They play incredible tennis. I think that French Open final was unreal. I think the game, not that it needed it, but it was great that we had it.”
Federer continued: “Then they backed it up by playing against each other in all those other finals. Everybody is trying to keep up and they’re trying to pull away. What we’ve seen in terms of their progression in the last years, it’s been wonderful.
“I practised with those guys a little bit. They’re incredible ball strikers. There’s obviously more to come. I just hope they stay injury free.”
Alcaraz will face Adam Walton of Australia in his first round match at Melbourne Park after the 2026 Australian Open draw ceremony was conducted on Thursday afternoon. Sinner, the second seed, will begin his title defence against France’s Hugo Gaston.
It remains to be seen if any challenger is capable of competing with either of the top two as both players chase historic victories in Melbourne.
Alcaraz, the men’s No 1, will be attempting to become the youngest man in history to win the career Grand Slam at just 22 years old. While Alcaraz has already won every other major twice, he is yet to pass the quarter-final stage at the Australian Open.
Sinner will attempt to win his third consecutive title in Melbourne. Seeded fourth, Novak Djokovic was drawn into Sinner’s half of the draw and will face Pedro Martinez in round one. Djokovic, who has won the Australian Open a record 10 times, will be attempting to put together another deep run and challenge for the title in his 22nd Grand Slam season.
The stakes are just as high in the women’s draw as Iga Świątek, the No 2 seed, will also be attempting to complete her career Grand Slam with a seventh major title overall, after previously winning four French Open titles and a title at Wimbledon and the US Open.
Swiatek, a semi-finalist in 2022, will begin her tournament against a qualifier, but she has been handed a difficult draw in the bottom half. The Pole could face Naomi Osaka, the 31st seed and two-time champion, the fifth seed, Elena Rybakina, and Amanda Anisimova, the fourth seed, in consecutive rounds.
At the top of the draw, world No 1, Aryna Sabalenka, seeks her third Australian Open title. Sabalenka’s loss in last year’s final to Madison Keys also marked her third consecutive final in Melbourne. The Belarusian, who won her 22nd WTA title in Brisbane last week, begins her Australian Open campaign against Sarah Rakotomang Rajaonah of France. – Guardian", "authors": "['Tumaini Carayol in Melbourne']", "description": "Six-time champion returns for opening ceremony in Melbourne as draws made"}

737:{"id": "43734e29935dc080ae3642c06f5d5e5050ec066709cc17981037b0654f054993", "title": "'Sincaraz' rivalry set to dominate Australian Open men's draw", "source": "www.straitstimes.com", "date": "2026-01-15", "date_publish": "2026-01-15", "date_modify": null, "url": "https://www.straitstimes.com/sport/tennis/sincaraz-rivalry-set-to-dominate-australian-open-mens-draw", "content": "MELBOURNE, Jan 15 - Defending champion Jannik Sinner is the bookmakers' favourite to win a third successive Australian Open crown as another title showdown looms with world number one Carlos Alcaraz.
The "Sincaraz" duopoly swept all four Grand Slam titles in 2025 and their dominance is expected to continue at Melbourne Park where few in the men's draw appear equipped to challenge, let alone beat them.
A Sinner victory would mean a fifth Grand Slam title for the 24-year-old Italian and make him the first to lift the Australian Open trophy three years running since Novak Djokovic in 2019-21.
For 22-year-old Alcaraz, a first Australian Open triumph would mean a seventh major trophy and the distinction of being the youngest man to complete the career Grand Slam, eclipsing Don Budge's long-standing record.
Sinner and Alcaraz have operated in a different stratosphere to hog the sport's biggest trophies, and even their travel arrangements to Melbourne were a class apart.
As lower-ranked players toiled through stifling heat at warm-up tournaments in Australia, the duo flew in on a private jet from a no-pressure exhibition match in Seoul, arriving fresh and unruffled for the tournament starting on Sunday.
While Alcaraz finished a career-best season in 2025 with the world's top ranking, Sinner capped his own terrific year by successfully defending his ATP Finals title, beating the Spaniard in the decider in front of adoring home fans in Turin.
The Italian, who faces France's Hugo Gaston in the first round, has worked to add greater unpredictability, particularly on serve, into his game to keep pace with Alcaraz, the sport's most explosive talent.
Alcaraz is determined to hit back in Melbourne, though, saying he would rather hoist the trophy than claim back-to-back titles at the U.S. and French Open this year.
Though dominant elsewhere, Australia has never seen Alcaraz in full flight, with quarter-final exits in the past two seasons his best results.
This year he arrives following a surprise split with long-time coach Juan Carlos Ferrero, who guided him from prodigy to multiple major champion.
Whether Alcaraz, who meets Australian battler Adam Walton in the first round, can continue to thrive without Ferrero will be one of the tournament's major plotlines.
Beyond the two young standard-bearers, Djokovic may still be their greatest threat -- even at 38. The Serb is chasing a record-extending 11th Australian Open title and a record 25th Grand Slam tit
[...truncated]

234:{"id": "6bfb823a4701de41c1ef93d8a15b1f7b474c3a58a0ec519b351758e88b5d259e", "title": "Emma Raducanu: British No 1 stunned by Taylah Preston at Hobart International quarter-finals ahead of Australian Open", "source": "www.skysports.com", "date": "2026-01-15", "date_publish": "2026-01-15", "date_modify": null, "url": "https://www.skysports.com/tennis/news/12040/13494480/emma-raducanu-british-no-1-stunned-by-taylah-preston-at-hobart-international-quarter-finals-ahead-of-australian-open", "content": "Emma Raducanu's preparations for the Australian Open took a hit after she was stunned 6-2 6-4 by Taylah Preston in the quarter-finals of the Hobart International.
Raducanu, who discovered she could meet world No 1 Aryna Sabalenka in the third round at the opening Grand Slam of the year following Thursday's draw, lacked firepower as Australia's Preston delivered an inspired performance on home soil.
It is just the second time Raducanu has lost to a player ranked outside the top 200 with Preston 204th in the WTA rankings ahead of this tournament.
Latest scores, results and schedule from WTA and ATP Tours
Australian Open 2026: Dates, seeds, format and favourites
Not got Sky? Get Sky Sports or stream contract-free on NOW
"I don't really know what to say! I'm just happy to get through to the next round and play in front of everyone," said 20-year-old Preston.
Raducanu made a flying start by breaking Preston in the opening game but the Australian broke back immediately, then held serve before a one-hour suspension for rain.
On resumption, it was all about Preston as she completely overpowered Raducanu and quickly went 4-1 up with another break and broke for a third time to win the first set in style.
Raducanu began a fightback with a break early in the second set as she stepped up the intensity only for Preston to find the lines again with more great ball-striking to break back and make it 3-3.
The British No 1 let another break advantage slip in the eighth game at 4-4 before Preston broke again to record the biggest win of her career and continue a dream week in Hobart.", "authors": "['Nigel Chiu']", "description": "Emma Raducanu was beaten in straight sets by Australias Taylah Preston, who is ranked 204th in the world, in a surprise defeat in the quarter-finals of the Hobart International; Raducanu will now travel to Melbourne for the Australian Open which begins on Sunday"}

266:{"id": "fdfdf0132ca2cc5b3a0921a82e2ea33814b797a8033a58d7ab0760b50e071958", "title": "Australian Open draw: Emma Raducanu in line to face Aryna Sabalenka in third round again while Novak Djokovic in same half as Jannik Sinner", "source": "www.skysports.com", "date": "2026-01-15", "date_publish": "2026-01-15", "date_modify": null, "url": "https://www.skysports.com/tennis/news/12040/13494465/australian-open-draw-emma-raducanu-in-line-to-face-aryna-sabalenka-in-third-round-again-while-novak-djokovic-in-same-half-as-jannik-sinner", "content": "Emma Raducanu has been handed a tough path at the Australian Open despite being seeded, with world No 1 Aryna Sabalenka potentially awaiting in the third round.
Raducanu, who lost in the quarter-finals of the Hobart International on Thursday, is seeded 28th for the opening Grand Slam of the year and will face Thailand's Mananchaya Sawangkaew in the first round.
Austria's Anastasia Potapova or Netherlands' Suzan Lamens would be Raducanu's second-round opponent before a rematch of last year's Wimbledon clash against Sabalenka, which also came in the third round.

Not got Sky? Get Sky Sports or stream contract-free on NOW
Pre-tournament favourite Sabalenka will begin her campaign for a third Australian Open title against French wild card Tiantsoa Rakotomanga Rajaonah while six-time major champion Iga Swiatek will play a qualifier, with a potential fourth-round meeting against Naomi Osaka in the second week.
Britain's Katie Boulter, who was a last-minute entry into the main draw, has a tough opener against in-form 10th seed Belinda Bencic, while Sonay Kartal faces 31st seed Anna Kalinskaya and Francesca Jones will play a qualifier.
Venus Williams is set to become the oldest woman to compete in an Australian Open main draw, surpassing the record of 44 years old, previously held by Japan's Kimiko Date, and could face compatriot Coco Gauff if the American duo win their opening matches.
Djokovic in same half as Sinner as Britain's Fery qualifies for main draw
Novak Djokovic has been drawn in the same half as defending men's singles champion Jannik Sinner as the Serbian continues his quest for a 25th Grand Slam.
Djokovic will enter the Australian Open, which he has won a record 10 times, without playing competitive tennis since November after withdrawing from last week's Adelaide International.
The 38-year-old cut short a practice session in Melbourne due to a neck problem on Wednesday, raising concerns about his fitness.
As the fourth seed, he cannot face Sinner until the semi-finals - the stage he lost at each of last year's four majors in 2025 - and will take on Spain's Pedro Martinez in his first match.
Reigning champion Sinner goes up against France's Hugo Gaston while Carlos Alcaraz, who is looking to complete his career Grand Slam, plays Australia's Adam Walton.
Britain's Cameron Norrie is seeded 26th and takes on France's Benjamin Bonzi, before a possible third-round tie with third seed Alexander Zverev, while Jacob Fearnley will meet Poland's Kamil Majchrzak.
Norrie and Fernley will be join in the main draw by compatriot Arthur Fery, who will make his maiden Grand Slam appearance outside of Wimbledon.
Fery won all three of his qualifying matches with straight-set victories and comfortably saw off Croatia's Dino
Prizmic 6-4 6-1 on Thursday and will take on Italy's Flavio Cobolli in his opening match.
Big Australian home hope Alex de Minaur has a tough first-round draw against former Wimbledon finalist Matteo Berrettini.", "authors": "[]", "description": "Emma Raducanu could meet Aryna Sabalenka in the third round for the second time in a Grand Slam in the last seven months following the Australian Open draw; watch WTA and ATP action in Adelaide and Hobart International this week, live on Sky Sports Tennis"}
651:{"id": "e4b21c0d1161daa82f600676afd1247d9fe9e66b5d2e723e9f01b98fca861e61", "title": "Roger Federer hails Alcaraz and Sinner’s ‘great’ rivalry amid Australian Open return", "source": "www.irishtimes.com", "date": "2026-01-15", "date_publish": "2026-01-15", "date_modify": null, "url": "https://www.irishtimes.com/sport/tennis/2026/01/15/roger-federer-hails-alcaraz-and-sinners-great-rivalry-amid-australian-open-return/", "content": "Roger Federer hailed the dominance of Carlos Alcaraz and Jannik Sinner as the six-time champion returned to the Australian Open for the first time since his retirement in 2022.
Federer, who last travelled to Australia in 2020, will headline the inaugural opening ceremony with a doubles exhibition match alongside Andre Agassi, Patrick Rafter and Lleyton Hewitt.
“The rivalry with Alcaraz and Sinner is a great one,” said Federer. “They play incredible tennis. I think that French Open final was unreal. I think the game, not that it needed it, but it was great that we had it.”
Federer continued: “Then they backed it up by playing against each other in all those other finals. Everybody is trying to keep up and they’re trying to pull away. What we’ve seen in terms of their progression in the last years, it’s been wonderful.
“I practised with those guys a little bit. They’re incredible ball strikers. There’s obviously more to come. I just hope they stay injury free.”
Alcaraz will face Adam Walton of Australia in his first round match at Melbourne Park after the 2026 Australian Open draw ceremony was conducted on Thursday afternoon. Sinner, the second seed, will begin his title defence against France’s Hugo Gaston.
It remains to be seen if any challenger is capable of competing with either of the top two as both players chase historic victories in Melbourne.
Alcaraz, the men’s No 1, will be attempting to become the youngest man in history to win the career Grand Slam at just 22 years old. While Alcaraz has already won every other major twice, he is yet to pass the quarter-final stage at the Australian Open.
Sinner will attempt to win his third consecutive title in Melbourne. Seeded fourth, Novak Djokovic was drawn into Sinner’s half of the draw and will face Pedro Martinez in round one. Djokovic, who has won the Australian Open a record 10 times, will be attempting to put together another deep run and challenge for the title in his 22nd Grand Slam season.
The stakes are just as high in the women’s draw as Iga Świątek, the No 2 seed, will also be attempting to complete her career Grand Slam with a seventh major title overall, after previously winning four French Open titles and a title at Wimbledon and the US Open.
Swiatek, a semi-finalist in 2022, will begin her tournament against a qualifier, but she has been handed a difficult draw in the bottom half. The Pole could face Naomi Osaka, the 31st seed and two-time champion, the fifth seed, Elena Rybakina, and Amanda Anisimova, the fourth seed, in consecutive rounds.
At the top of the draw, world No 1, Aryna Sabalenka, seeks her third Australian Open title. Sabalenka’s loss in last year’s final to Madison Keys also marked her third consecutive final in Melbourne. The Belarusian, who won her 22nd WTA title in Brisbane last week, begins her Australian Open campaign against Sarah Rakotomang Rajaonah of France. – Guardian", "authors": "['Tumaini Carayol in Melbourne']", "description": "Six-time champion returns for opening ceremony in Melbourne as draws made"}

Austria's Anastasia Potapova or Netherlands' Suzan Lamens would be Raducanu's second-round opponent before a rematch of last year's Wimbledon clash against Sabalenka, which also came in the third round.

Venus Williams is set to become the oldest woman to compete in an Australian Open main draw, surpassing the record of 44 years old, previously held by Japan's Kimiko Date, and could face compatriot Coco Gauff if the American duo win their opening matches.
Djokovic in same half as Sinner as Britain's Fery qualifies for main draw
Novak Djokovic has been drawn in the same half as defending men's singles champion Jannik Sinner as the Serbian continues his quest for a 25th Grand Slam.
Djokovic will enter the Australian Open, which he has won a record 10 times, without playing competitive tennis since November after withdrawing from last week's Adelaide International.
The 38-year-old cut short a practice session in Melbourne due to a neck problem on Wednesday, raising concerns about his fitness.
As the fourth seed, he cannot face Sinner until the semi-finals - the stage he lost at each of last year's four majors in 2025 - and will take on Spain's Pedro Martinez in his first match.
Reigning champion Sinner goes up against France's Hugo Gaston while Carlos Alcaraz, who is looking to complete his career Grand Slam, plays Australia's Adam Walton.
Britain's Cameron Norrie is seeded 26th and takes on France's Benjamin Bonzi, before a possible third-round tie with third seed Alexander Zverev, while Jacob Fearnley will meet Poland's Kamil Majchrzak.
Norrie and Fernley will be join in the main draw by compatriot Arthur Fery, who will make his maiden Grand Slam appearance outside of Wimbledon.
Fery won all three of his qualifying matches with straight-set victories and comfortably saw off Croatia's Dino
Prizmic 6-4 6-1 on Thursday and will take on Italy's Flavio Cobolli in his opening match.

Federer, who last travelled to Australia in 2020, will headline the inaugural opening ceremony with a doubles exhibition match alongside Andre Agassi, Patrick Rafter and Lleyton Hewitt.
“The rivalry with Alcaraz and Sinner is a great one,” said Federer. “They play incredible tennis. I think that French Open final was unreal. I think the game, not that it needed it, but it was great that we had it.”
Federer continued: “Then they backed it up by playing against each other in all those other finals. Everybody is trying to keep up and they’re trying to pull away. What we’ve seen in terms of their progression in the last years, it’s been wonderful.
“I practised with those guys a little bit. They’re incredible ball strikers. There’s obviously more to come. I just hope they stay injury free.”
Alcaraz will face Adam Walton of Australia in his first round match at Melbourne Park after the 2026 Australian Open draw ceremony was conducted on Thursday afternoon. Sinner, the second seed, will begin his title defence against France’s Hugo Gaston.
It remains to be seen if any challenger is capable of competing with either of the top two as both players chase historic victories in Melbourne.
Alcaraz, the men’s No 1, will be attempting to become the youngest man in history to win the career Grand Slam at just 22 years old. While Alcaraz has already won every other major twice, he is yet to pass the quarter-final stage at the Australian Open.
Sinner will attempt to win his third consecutive title in Melbourne. Seeded fourth, Novak Djokovic was drawn into Sinner’s half of the draw and will face Pedro Martinez in round one. Djokovic, who has won the Australian Open a record 10 times, will be attempting to put together another deep run and challenge for the title in his 22nd Grand Slam season.
The stakes are just as high in the women’s draw as Iga Świątek, the No 2 seed, will also be attempting to complete her career Grand Slam with a seventh major title overall, after previously winning four French Open titles and a title at Wimbledon and the US Open.
Swiatek, a semi-finalist in 2022, will begin her tournament against a qualifier, but she has been handed a difficult draw in the bottom half. The Pole could face Naomi Osaka, the 31st seed and two-time champion, the fifth seed, Elena Rybakina, and Amanda Anisimova, the fourth seed, in consecutive rounds.
At the top of the draw, world No 1, Aryna Sabalenka, seeks her third Australian Open title. Sabalenka’s loss in last year’s final to Madison Keys also marked her third consecutive final in Melbourne. The Belarusian, who won her 22nd WTA title in Brisbane last week, begins her Australian Open campaign against Sarah Rakotomang Rajaonah of France. – Guardian", "authors": "['Tumaini Carayol in Melbourne']", "description": "Six-time champion returns for opening ceremony in Melbourne as draws made"}

The "Sincaraz" duopoly swept all four Grand Slam titles in 2025 and their dominance is expected to continue at Melbourne Park where few in the men's draw appear equipped to challenge, let alone beat them.
A Sinner victory would mean a fifth Grand Slam title for the 24-year-old Italian and make him the first to lift the Australian Open trophy three years running since Novak Djokovic in 2019-21.
For 22-year-old Alcaraz, a first Australian Open triumph would mean a seventh major trophy and the distinction of being the youngest man to complete the career Grand Slam, eclipsing Don Budge's long-standing record.
Sinner and Alcaraz have operated in a different stratosphere to hog the sport's biggest trophies, and even their travel arrangements to Melbourne were a class apart.
As lower-ranked players toiled through stifling heat at warm-up tournaments in Australia, the duo flew in on a private jet from a no-pressure exhibition match in Seoul, arriving fresh and unruffled for the tournament starting on Sunday.
While Alcaraz finished a career-best season in 2025 with the world's top ranking, Sinner capped his own terrific year by successfully defending his ATP Finals title, beating the Spaniard in the decider in front of adoring home fans in Turin.
The Italian, who faces France's Hugo Gaston in the first round, has worked to add greater unpredictability, particularly on serve, into his game to keep pace with Alcaraz, the sport's most explosive talent.
Alcaraz is determined to hit back in Melbourne, though, saying he would rather hoist the trophy than claim back-to-back titles at the U.S. and French Open this year.
Though dominant elsewhere, Australia has never seen Alcaraz in full flight, with quarter-final exits in the past two seasons his best results.
This year he arrives following a surprise split with long-time coach Juan Carlos Ferrero, who guided him from prodigy to multiple major champion.
Whether Alcaraz, who meets Australian battler Adam Walton in the first round, can continue to thrive without Ferrero will be one of the tournament's major plotlines.
Beyond the two young standard-bearers, Djokovic may still be their greatest threat -- even at 38. The Serb is chasing a record-extending 11th Australian Open title and a record 25th Grand Slam tit

Jan 15 - India-born Dilpreet Bajwa will lead Canada at the Twenty20 World Cup next month, the country's cricket board said on Wednesday as it announced a 15-member squad for the competition. Canada, making their second appearance at the tournament, are in Group D alongside South Africa, New Zealand, Afghanistan, and the United Arab Emirates. Their campaign opens against South Africa on February 9 in Ahmedabad, with all their group-stage matches set to be played in India. Bajwa, who has four half-centuries and a strike rate of 133.22 in T20Is, will be seen alongside power-hitter Yuvraj Samra, former captain Saad Bin Zafar, Dilon Heyliger, Nicholas Kirton and Navneet Dhaliwal among others in the squad. Canada secured their spot in the tournament by winning all six of their matches in the Americas Regional Final. In their previous World Cup appearance, Canada finished fourth in their group, with a single win over Ireland. HEADLINE: Hamas welcomes technocratic committee as key to consolidating Gaza truce
SOURCE: www.straitstimes.com

Hamas welcomes technocratic committee as key to consolidating Gaza truce

GAZA CITY, Palestinian Territories – A senior Hamas official on Jan 15 welcomed the formation of a technocratic committee to govern post-war Gaza, saying it would help consolidate the ceasefire and prevent a return to fighting. Egypt, a mediator in indirect ceasefire talks between Israel and Hamas, announced the formation of a 15-member Palestinian technocratic committee that would operate under the overall supervision of a so-called “Board of Peace”, to be chaired by US President Donald Trump. “The formation of the committee is a step in the right direction,” said Mr Bassem Naim, a senior leader in the Palestinian Islamist movement. “This is crucial for consolidating the ceasefire, preventing a return to war, addressing the catastrophic humanitarian crisis and preparing for comprehensive reconstruction,” he said. Hamas has exercised total control over public life in Gaza since 2007, and it was its October 2023 attack on Israel that sparked the Gaza war. It has repeatedly said it does not seek a role in any future governing authority in Gaza and would limit its involvement to monitoring governance. Mr Naim said Hamas was ready to hand over administration of the Gaza Strip to the national transitional committee and to facilitate its work. “The ball is now in the court of the mediators, the American guarantor and the international community to empower the committee,” he said. He urged them to counter what he described as Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu’s attempts to “stall or obstruct” the next stages of the ceasefire. Since
the ceasefire took effect on Oct 10,
Gaza has been split by a so-called “Yellow Line”, marking the boundary between territory controlled by Hamas and areas under Israeli military authority. Washington’s top envoy, Mr Steve Witkoff, said on Jan 14 the ceasefire had moved into its second stage.

HEADLINE: Who is Nickolay Mladenov, the diplomat tasked with ‘disarming Gaza’?
SOURCE: www.aljazeera.com
PUBLISHED: 2026-01-15 00:00:00 | DOWNLOADED: 2026-01-15 00:00:00
URL: https://www.aljazeera.com/features/2026/1/15/who-is-nickolay-mladenov-the-diplomat-tasked-with-disarming-gaza

Who is Nickolay Mladenov, the diplomat tasked with ‘disarming Gaza’?

The search for a figure to lead post-war Gaza, which lies in ruins from Israel’s genocidal war, has moved from diplomatic backrooms to the negotiating tables in Cairo. Following the Arab veto of the regionally toxic former British leader Tony Blair, Washington has deployed its Plan B, Nickolay Mladenov, as the push for phase two of the fragile ceasefire gains some momentum. The 53-year-old former Bulgarian foreign minister and defence minister is no longer just a nominee; he is arguably the most critical figure in the newly launched phase two of the ceasefire, which Israel has violated on a daily basis since October 10. Mladenov has been confirmed as the director-general of the United States-proposed “Board of Peace”. His mandate is to oversee the transition from Hamas rule to a new technocratic administration led by Ali Shaath, a former Palestinian Authority (PA) deputy minister. For five years from 2015-2020, Mladenov served as the United Nations’ top envoy to the region, earning a reputation as a “firefighter” who could talk to everyone. Now, he returns with a far more fraught and potentially explosive mission: Implementing a US-designed plan that explicitly calls for the “disarmament of all unauthorised personnel” – a euphemism for ending Hamas’s military power while Israel continues its occupation. The mediator’s test

Egypt, a mediator in indirect ceasefire talks between Israel and Hamas, announced the formation of a 15-member Palestinian technocratic committee that would operate under the overall supervision of a so-called “Board of Peace”, to be chaired by US President Donald Trump. “The formation of the committee is a step in the right direction,” said Mr Bassem Naim, a senior leader in the Palestinian Islamist movement. “This is crucial for consolidating the ceasefire, preventing a return to war, addressing the catastrophic humanitarian crisis and preparing for comprehensive reconstruction,” he said. blocks.content:
Watched but no update:
- Ukraine opposition bribery, pro-Israel New York settlement, Atletico Copa opponent, Southeast Asian visa pause, NE ICE crackdown state, food-aid halt, basketball-rigging league, migrant-worker custody death, Syria language/holiday, Marc Guehi, Ukraine combat planes, Indonesian plane operator, Karachi fire, Portugal runoff, and the Australian Open opponent markets had no direct Jan. 11 naming signal strong enough to move.
- Al-Shaddadi remains a Syrian army lean, but the Aleppo withdrawal does not directly identify who will control that base.

## 2026-01-12 Update

Resolution events:
- Ukrainian opposition bribery resolved to Yulia Tymoshenko. I overweighted Poroshenko and treated old opposition leaders as tails. Lesson: when the clue only says "opposition leader" in Ukraine, keep Tymoshenko materially live even if current reporting is thin.
- The pro-Israel New York settlement resolved to Betar US. I had Betar USA, which is likely an exact-string miss. Lesson: use the organization's exact published short name when known, even if a close variant is semantically obvious.
- Atletico Madrid Copa opponent resolved to Real Betis. I missed the draw by leaning Sevilla/Real Sociedad. Lesson: sports draw markets need the actual draw when available; otherwise include plausible domestic rivals like Betis, not only the biggest names.

Updates made:
- Marc Guehi: Sky said the race remains wide open and specifically added Bayern Munich as the viable foreign pre-contract route, while the Independent said Manchester City are actively pushing a January deal and Guehi is open to it. Kept City first but added Bayern as a serious hedge.
- Oscars nominations: Golden Globes coverage said One Battle After Another led the Globe nominations and won major film awards; Hamnet won drama and Sinners remains a high-nomination contender. Raised One Battle After Another and Paul Thomas Anderson in the linked Oscar markets while adding Sentimental Value/Joachim Trier as nonzero nomination-count hedges.
- Greenland framework: Jan. 12 coverage centered the upcoming Washington talks on Greenland FM Vivian Motzfeldt, Danish FM Lars Lokke Rasmussen, Mette Frederiksen's "decisive moment" warning, and Rubio. Kept Motzfeldt first, raised Mette and Lars slightly, and trimmed Jens-Frederik Nielsen.
- Gaza body name: AP-style Jan. 12 coverage used the phrase "Palestinian technocratic leadership committee" and said Hamas would dissolve its Gaza government once that body takes over. Added that exact wording above the shorter Palestinian Technocratic Committee to reduce exact-name risk, with Board of Peace still a main alternative because it oversees the body.

Watched but no update:
- Gaza committee leader: the same AP-style report said the technocrats' names have not been announced. Mladenov remains the only named director/CEO-type figure from Board of Peace reporting, but the article does not directly name the Palestinian committee leader.
- Iran escalation reporting was much hotter, with Trump saying the military is weighing strong options and Iran warning US bases/ships could be targets. It reinforces the existing Qatar/Bahrain/Iraq base-location priors but did not identify a specific base, talks venue, or strike site enough to change those detailed markets.
- NE ICE crackdown state, Southeast Asian visa pause, Massie/Venezuelan oil-proceeds country, Venus/Australian Open draw, UN food-aid halt, basketball-rigging league, migrant-worker custody death, Syria holiday/language, Ukraine combat planes, Indonesian plane operator, Karachi fire, Portugal runoff, Morocco missed penalty, Bangladesh election-party and T20-replacement markets had no stronger Jan. 12 naming signal.

## 2026-01-13 Update

Resolution events:
- NE ICE crackdown resolved to Maine. I had only New York/Massachusetts. Lesson: for northeastern enforcement-market tails, include Maine/New Hampshire/Vermont when the wording says "northeastern state" and not just the largest sanctuary-state fights.
- Gaza committee leader resolved to Ali Shaath. I missed by over-weighting Mladenov/Board of Peace and PA figures. Lesson: for "Palestinian technocratic committee" leadership, add less famous Gaza technocrats and local civil-administration names, not only national politicians or international overseers.
- Massie Venezuelan oil proceeds resolved to Qatar, not the United States. Lesson: offshore/escrow oil-money questions can resolve to mediator/gulf-finance states even when US control is the policy frame.
- Southeast Asian treaty-ally visa pause resolved to Thailand. Lesson: do not assume Philippines for every US treaty-ally immigration action; Thailand should be a core hedge.
- Gaza body name resolved to Palestinian national committee. I chased "technocratic leadership committee" and missed the simpler national-committee phrase. Lesson: for Gaza governance bodies, include "Palestinian national committee" alongside technocratic variants.

Updates made:
- Bangladesh cricket: Al Jazeera and Hindustan Times reported that the BCB reaffirmed it will not travel to India for T20 World Cup matches and asked the ICC to relocate Bangladesh's fixtures. Raised Bangladesh for the Pakistan-boycott-support market and raised UAE modestly for a possible Bangladesh replacement team.
- Gaza/Board of Peace participation: Hindustan Times reported Bangladesh raised joining the Gaza international force in State Department meetings and received a positive US response, with conditions attached; it also mentioned Pakistan participation. Raised Bangladesh for the only South Asian Board of Peace member and added Bangladesh to the later "up to 8,000 troops" Gaza-mission market.
- Minnesota/DOJ governor: Jan. 13 coverage had DHS directly demanding Gov. Tim Walz and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey honor ICE detainers, while Minnesota sued over the ICE surge and Trump attacked Walz. Moved Walz above Pritzker for the DOJ investigation/conspiracy-to-impede-enforcement market.
- Carrick/Manchester United: Carrick was confirmed as United interim head coach, with his first match the Manchester derby and Arsenal away soon after. Raised Manchester City and Arsenal for Carrick's first-defeat market.
- NFL playoffs: Eagles were out, and the NFC divisional field is Seahawks, Bears, 49ers and Rams. Removed Hurts/Eagles exposure, raised Sam Darnold, Caleb Williams, Brock Purdy and Matthew Stafford for the NFC Super Bowl quarterback market, and updated the Super Bowl winner set toward Seahawks/Broncos/Bears/Patriots/49ers.
- Greenland framework: Daily Beast and other coverage said JD Vance will host the White House meeting with the Danish and Greenland foreign ministers, while Greenland PM Jens-Frederik Nielsen emphasized NATO/Denmark. Raised Vivian Motzfeldt and Lars Lokke Rasmussen as direct-meeting counterparts while keeping Mette/Jens live.
- Syria/SDF assault: Jan. 13 Al Jazeera/SANA coverage said the Syrian army sent troops toward eastern Aleppo after observing SDF reinforcements near Maskana and Deir Hafer. Kept Aleppo first but added Maskana/Deir Hafer hedges for the assault-target city.
- Iran diplomacy/tariffs: Trump announced a 25% tariff on countries doing business with Iran, with reporting naming China first/top and also UAE/Turkey/India. Moved China above India for the tariff-country market. Qatari PM contact with an Iranian official and Doha de-escalation language raised Qatar/Doha modestly for Iran talks/intermediary markets, while Oman/Muscat remains first for formal indirect talks.
- Venus Williams: Sky reported Venus lost to Tatjana Maria at Hobart less than a week before the Australian Open. Added Tatjana Maria as a live hedge for the AO first-round defeater market, while recognizing the AO draw is not yet available.
- Iran World Cup participation: escalating protests, US citizen departure warnings, tariff threats and military-option coverage increase the chance Iran cites inability to participate or seeks venue changes. Raised Iran for the "cannot participate in World Cup" market, but kept the detailed venue/strike/base markets mostly unchanged without location-specific reporting.

Watched but no update:
- Jan. 14 basketball-rigging league, migrant-worker custody death, UN emergency food/nutrition aid halt, Ukraine combat-plane provider, Syria national language/holiday, UDP New Jersey primary target, Indonesian plane operator, Karachi fire and southern Spain train-collision markets still had no direct naming signal.
- Marc Guehi coverage repeated the Jan. 12 Sky state of play; no new club agreement signal beyond the existing City/Bayern/Liverpool/Arsenal distribution.
- Sudan RSF drone attack in Sinja killed 27 at an army base, but it did not match the later market wording about a crowded central-Sudan market killing at least 28.

## 2026-01-14 Update

Resolution events:
- "Big armada" resolved to Middle East, not Caribbean. I overweighted Venezuela/naval-seizure coverage and missed the Iran/US regional-buildup frame. Lesson: when wording is broad and US-Iran tension is hot, keep Middle East/Gulf as a core region even if Caribbean military coverage is vivid.
- Foreign basketball league targeted in the game-rigging scheme resolved to Chinese Basketball Association. I had it in the set but too low. Lesson: in US sports-corruption cases, Chinese Basketball Association is a strong foreign-league prior.
- Migrant-worker custody death resolved to El Hacen Diarra. I missed the exact name. Lesson: for custody-death/protest markets, include local/French-speaking West African names where available rather than only English-language protest names.
- UN emergency food/nutrition aid halt resolved to Somalia. I had Somalia but behind Sudan. Lesson: for WFP/UN emergency nutrition funding-shortage wording, Somalia should be near the top even when Sudan and Gaza dominate humanitarian headlines.
- Venus Williams AO first-round defeater resolved to Olga Danilovic. My Tatjana Maria warmup hedge did not transfer to the AO draw. Lesson: tennis draw markets need bracket names; do not overfit tune-up opponents before the draw.

Updates made:
- Syria/SDF: Jan. 14 reporting said the army's evacuation corridor appeared to signal an offensive in Deir Hafer and Maskana east of Aleppo. Moved the SDF assault target from Aleppo toward Deir Hafer/Maskana, and reduced but kept Aleppo for the complete-control follow-on market.
- Al-Shaddadi: the same pressure on SDF withdrawal/integration keeps Syrian army first for eventual base control, but SDF remains a meaningful holdout tail.
- Iran/Gulf bases: Al Udeid personnel in Qatar were advised to leave after Iran warned regional states that US bases could be attacked. Raised Qatar across the killed-troops, KC-135, launch-area, embassy-evacuation and missile-recipient markets, with Bahrain/UAE/Saudi Arabia as main alternatives.
- Strait/Hormuz conflict risk: the hotter Iran-US confrontation raises Strait of Hormuz/Gulf of Oman risk for projectile-hit cargo shipping.
- Greenland framework: White House meetings featured Greenland and Danish foreign ministers, so moved Lars Lokke Rasmussen up while keeping Vivian Motzfeldt first and Mette/Jens as hedges.
- League Cup: Arsenal beat Chelsea 3-2 and Manchester City beat Newcastle 2-0 in first legs. Raised Arsenal sharply as City's likely final opponent.
- Portugal: the Catolica poll showed Ventura 24, Antonio Jose Seguro 23, Joao Cotrim de Figueiredo 19, and Marques Mendes/Gouveia e Melo around 14. Moved Seguro first for the runoff opponent while keeping Cotrim and the two conservative/independent candidates live.
- DOJ/governor immigration market: Jan. 14 ICE/Renee Good coverage kept Minnesota and Tim Walz central to the federal confrontation, with DOJ still refusing/contesting investigations. Raised Walz over Pritzker.
- Marc Guehi: Jan. 14 transfer live coverage said he prefers Arsenal or Liverpool, City are desperate for January, and Bayern are interested. Kept City first but spread more to Liverpool/Arsenal/Bayern.
- Ukraine-Russia-US talks: reports and EU commentary kept US-led negotiations centered on Witkoff/Trump channels while Europe debates direct Russia engagement. Added Moscow as the leading city hedge for the Jan. 23-24 three-way-talks market, but kept US mediation first.

Watched but no update:
- Syria national language/holiday, Ukraine combat-plane provider, UDP New Jersey target, Indonesian plane operator, Karachi fire, Morocco missed-penalty final market, Australian Open draw markets, Gaza child evacuation, Board of Peace South Asian member, Gaza troop provider, tariff-country, ICE/Olympics, LA wildfire funds and Super Bowl markets had no Jan. 14 naming signal strong enough to move beyond existing distributions.

## 2026-01-15 Update

Resolution events:
- Syria holiday resolved to Newroz. I had Nowruz but not the exact Kurdish spelling. Lesson: for Kurdish/Syrian holiday questions, use "Newroz" as a core exact-string outcome alongside Nowruz.
- Marc Guehi agreed-in-principle club resolved to Manchester City, which matched the City-first January transfer signal.
- Syria national language resolved to Kurdish, matching the main distribution.
- DOJ immigration-conspiracy governor resolved to Tim Walz, matching the Minnesota/ICE escalation signal.
- Ukraine combat planes resolved to Czech Republic, not Sweden or France. Lesson: for "shortly provide combat planes" markets, include Czech Republic as a serious European aircraft-transfer tail, not only Gripen/Mirage/F-16 headline countries.

Updates made:
- Australian Open draw: Sky listed the full singles draw. Replaced pre-draw tennis guesses with bracket-based forecasts: Alcaraz's R2 path is Yannick Hanfmann vs Zachary Svajda; Sinner's R2 path is James Duckworth vs Dino Prizmic; Alcaraz's likely R4 opponent is Tommy Paul or Alejandro Davidovich Fokina; Sabalenka's R3/RQF sections point to Raducanu/Potapova and then Paolini/Alexandrova/Kostyuk/Tauson; Osaka's R3 section points to Liudmila Samsonova or Laura Siegemund; Keys' likely eliminators are now in Pegula/Badosa/Fernandez territory. Also nudged the men's title market slightly toward Alcaraz after Sinner and Djokovic landed in the same half and Djokovic showed a neck concern.
- Syria/SDF: Al Jazeera reported a humanitarian corridor for residents to leave Deir Hafer and Maskana, with the army declaring those areas military zones and a reporter in Deir Hafer describing a heavily armed convoy. Raised Deir Hafer and Maskana sharply for the SDF assault-target city and reduced Aleppo in the follow-on complete-control market.
- Greenland: multiple Jan. 15 articles said Lars Lokke Rasmussen and Vivian Motzfeldt left the White House meeting with a "fundamental disagreement" but a high-level US-Danish/Greenland working group. Moved Lars slightly ahead of Motzfeldt for a possible Trump "deal framework" counterpart.
- Ukraine/Russia talks: Reuters/Straits Times said the Kremlin would welcome Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner to the Kremlin for further Ukraine talks once a date is agreed. Raised Moscow for the Jan. 23-24 three-way talks and added Moscow to the Feb. 4 second-round talks market.
- Bangladesh cricket: Jan. 15 Al Jazeera/Hindustan Times coverage said Bangladesh players boycotted BPL matches, the BCB sacked director Nazmul Islam, and Bangladesh's India venue dispute remains live. Raised Bangladesh further for Pakistan's support-by-boycott country and nudged UAE higher as a possible Bangladesh T20 replacement.
- AFCON: Morocco beat Nigeria on penalties to set up the final with Senegal; Youssef En-Nesyri scored the decisive shootout kick. Kept Brahim Diaz first for the stoppage-time missed-penalty market but raised Hakimi and En-Nesyri.
